extra.reasoning:
Property: NOT(rec_drug_a AND rec_drug_b). Let C = (asthma AND elderly AND pregnancy AND seizure_history AND prior_gi_bleed AND immunosuppressed).

rec_drug_a: The only rule setting it true is rec_a_from_conjunction (priority 50) which fires exactly when C holds; otherwise the default sets it false. So rec_drug_a = true iff C is true.

rec_drug_b: Three relevant rules. guard_suppress_b (priority 90) fires when C holds and sets rec_drug_b := false. rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50) fires when asthma holds and sets rec_drug_b := true. Default is false. Conflict resolution is highest-priority-wins per output.

Critical interaction: rec_drug_a can only be true when C is true. But C implies asthma, so when C is true BOTH guard_suppress_b (priority 90) and rec_b_from_trigger (priority 50) match for rec_drug_b. The priority-90 guard wins over the priority-50 rule, forcing rec_drug_b := false. Therefore whenever rec_drug_a = true (i.e., C true), rec_drug_b is forced false by the guard. They can never be jointly true.

Conversely, when rec_drug_b = true, asthma is true but C is false (otherwise the guard would suppress b), and with C false rec_drug_a defaults to false. No conflict either way. The guard at priority 90 is precisely engineered to enforce this mutual exclusion. The property holds over the entire input space.
